parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Authenticate as normal user 2) PATCH own or another user's ID with {"isAdmin":true} 3) Privilege escalated
parsed_findings.impact: Full privilege escalation; attacker can grant themselves admin or alter any other user attribute.
parsed_findings.proof: Object.assign(user, req.body) merges all client-supplied keys onto the DB model before saving.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/users/:id with body {"isAdmin":true} to escalate privileges.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass assignment allows attacker to overwrite any user field including privileged ones (e.g. isAdmin, role).

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Login as user A 2) PATCH /api/users/<B> with arbitrary body 3) User B's record is overwritten
parsed_findings.impact: Any authenticated user can overwrite another user's data (IDOR).
parsed_findings.proof: Condition `req.user.id ... req.user.isSupport` (likely || or &&) does not enforce that req.user.id === req.params.id.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate as user A, then PATCH /api/users/<user_B_id> — no ownership check prevents cross-user modification.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authorization check only blocks if BOTH conditions fail; authenticated non-support users can modify any other user's record.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) PATCH /api/users/__proto__ 2) body {"isAdmin":true} 3) Object.assign propagates to Object.prototype
parsed_findings.impact: Denial of service or prototype pollution affecting all subsequent Object.prototype lookups server-wide.
parsed_findings.proof: findById with a crafted id (e.g. __proto__) returns null/unexpected object; Object.assign(null, body) throws or pollutes prototype.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/users/__proto__ with body {"isAdmin":true} to attempt prototype pollution or crash the process.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No validation that req.params.id references an existing user; a null/undefined user object passed to Object.assign causes unhandled exception or prototype pollution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Read balance concurrently 2) Both pass src<amt check 3) Both decrement balance
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker drains account below zero, creating funds from thin air.
parsed_findings.proof: Two simultaneous requests both read src before either write, bypassing the balance check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two concurrent POST /transfer requests for the full balance; both pass the check and both debit.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Race condition (TOCTOU) allows double-spend via concurrent requests.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Send negative amount 2) Balance check passes 3) src-(-100) increases sender balance
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker mints arbitrary funds by transferring negative amounts to any target.
parsed_findings.proof: int(request.json['amount']) with amt=-100 passes src<amt check (src>=0 > -100) and adds funds to sender.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with amount=-100; src(0) < -100 is false so transfer proceeds, crediting attacker.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No validation on amount allows negative or zero values enabling reverse transfers.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Supply arbitrary to value 2) No existence/permission check 3) Balance credited to target
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can probe or credit arbitrary account IDs including admin accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: to is taken directly from user input with no validation that the recipient account exists or is permitted.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with to=<any_user_id>; funds move without verifying recipient legitimacy.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No authorization check on recipient 'to' field allows transferring to arbitrary accounts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Craft JWT with forged claims signed by attacker key. 2) Send request to production endpoint. 3) verify() throws, decode() returns forged payload. 4) Caller trusts returned object as authenticated identity.
parsed_findings.impact: Authentication bypass — attacker can forge arbitrary claims including admin roles.
parsed_findings.proof: If JWT_PUBLIC_KEY is missing or the token is expired, the catch block calls jwt.decode() which returns the payload without verifying the signature, allowing forged tokens.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a JWT signed with an attacker-controlled key; the verify() call throws, and in production the unverified payload is trusted.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: jwt.decode() bypasses signature verification in production on any JWT error

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Measure timing of == comparison across signature candidates 2) Recover signature byte-by-byte 3) Forge valid requests
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can brute-force the valid HMAC signature over many requests, bypassing webhook authentication entirely.

parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send requests with signatures differing at each byte position and measure response latency to recover the expected HMAC one byte at a time.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Timing side-channel in HMAC comparison via non-constant-time string equality

parsed_findings.impact: Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference; potential code execution if attacker controls memory layout.
parsed_findings.proof: if (out || out_sz == 0) — when out==NULL, `out` is falsy so condition is false; execution reaches memcpy(NULL, in, n).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: call copy_name("hello", NULL, 10); guard passes, memcpy(NULL,...) crashes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: NULL pointer dereference due to inverted guard: `out ||` should be `!out ||`, allowing NULL out to pass the check.

results.raw_output:
Line 3 has an inverted null-check: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` should be `if (!out || out_sz == 0)`. When `out` is NULL, the condition is false (NULL is falsy), so execution continues and `memcpy` writes to a null pointer.

```json
{"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"NULL pointer dereference due to inverted guard: `out ||` should be `!out ||`, allowing NULL out to pass the check.","proof":"if (out || out_sz == 0) — when out==NULL, `out` is falsy so condition is false; execution reaches memcpy(NULL, in, n).","repro_steps":"call copy_name(\"hello\", NULL, 10); guard passes, memcpy(NULL,...) crashes.","impact":"Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference; potential code execution if attacker controls memory layout.","confidence":0.97,"chain_steps":""}]}
```
results.stdout_preview:
Line 3 has an inverted null-check: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` should be `if (!out || out_sz == 0)`. When `out` is NULL, the condition is false (NULL is falsy), so execution continues and `memcpy` writes to a null pointer.
